titleStmt.title: The Acting Secretary of State to the Ambassador in France ( (Edge))

div.head: The Acting Secretary of State to the Ambassador in France ( Edge )
div.p: 328. It is our opinion that the time has now come when if a definite acceptance is not obtained tomorrow from the French, you should consider very seriously whether it may not be appropriate then to hand them the second proposal of the President, which was set forth in our telegram No. 314, June 30, 11 p.m. Two weeks have gone by since Mr. Hoover offered on behalf of the people of the United States to give up for 1 year all payments due the American Government on intergovernmental debts on condition that the principal creditor nations would consent to a similar suspension on debts due them. The proposal’s principal merits were its comprehensiveness, its simplicity, and the fact that it could be applied immediately. The long-drawn-out negotiations with the French Government involving technicalities and details of application have clearly shown that if all such incidental and collateral matters have to be decided on before the general proposal is accepted and applied, its primary object will have been lost.

The discussion, which seems to be without end, is leading to no satisfactory results, and conditions in Germany are going from bad to worse without let-up. Consequently, in the hope of saving something from the muddle, President Hoover has under consideration making his second proposition on Monday morning, July 6.
